coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and experimental investigation of δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite for Li-O2 batteries applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zahoor, Awan
Faizan, Raza
Elsaid, Khaled
Hashmi, Saud
Butt, Faaz Ahmed
Ghouri, Zafar Khan
coredata.dc:description: Among all the electrocatalysts for Lithium-Air battery (Li-air), Platinum Pt is the best performing cathode material. However, the high cost of noble Pt metal and scarcity nature impedes the use of Pt-based catalysts from being extensivity used in commercial applications. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an efficient and cost-effective alternate electrocatalyst to replace Pt-based materials in lithium-oxygen (Li-O2) battaries. In the present work, δ-MnO2/N-rGO composite (MNGC) has been synthesized by a simple non-template hydrothermal approach. MNGC with a porous wall structure composed of ultrathin nanosheets exhibits excellent electrochemical properties for oxidation–reduction reaction (ORR). MNGC can provide numerous pathways for abundant oxygen and electrolyte access to facilitate the mass transfer of lithium-ion. Such a well-designed structure offers the right electrocatalyst for the air cathode in lithium-oxygen (Li-O2) battaries. The prepared samples principal characteristics are analyzed, which verified the successful synthesis of sheet-like δ-MnO2 grown over the surface of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) results of MNGC showed enhanced ORR performance compared to MnO2 and N-rGO in terms of the half-wave potential, limiting current, and onset potential. MNGC electrode displayed superior cyclic performances of Li-air with a stable specific capacity, decreased overpotential, reversibility, and rate capability. Li-O2 battery was also tested with MNGN electrode for limited discharge capacity of 500 mAh/g, long-term cycling was achieved without electrolyte degradation. A high specific capacity of 5250 mAh/g was obtained at a high current density of 0.2 mA/cm2. The interlinked effect of δ–MnO2 and N–rGO for supporting the electrochemical interaction between O2 and Li is explained for improved columbic and energy efficiency of Li–O2 battery
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium air battery
MnO2
Retention capacity
N–rGO
Columbic efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The XRD patterns of GO, N-rGO, δ -MnO2 and δ -MnO2/N-rGO composite and (B) RAMAN spectrum of GO and N-rGO.
The XPS spectrum of δ -MnO2/N-rGO composite (A) Survey spectrum and high-resolution spectra for (B) C1s, (C) O1s and (D) N1s species.
SEM images of (A) GO (B) N-rGO (C and D) low and high magnification image of δ -MnO2/N-rGO composite, respectively.

(A). SEM image (B) corresponding EDX map (inset) weight and elemental percentage of C, O, N, and Mn species and (C) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (inset) BJH pore size distribution of δ -MnO2/N-rGO composite.
(A) Cyclic Voltammograms (CV) curves in O2 saturated in 0.1 M KOH solution (B) Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) curves of MnO2, N-rGO and δ -MnO2/N-rGO composite.

(A) First cycle capacities of δ-MnO2/N-rGO electrode at 0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 mA/cm2 (B) Charge–discharge curves of δ-MnO2/N-rGO cathode at 0.1 mA/cm2 (C) specific capacity and efficiency with respect to cycle number of δ-MnO2/N-rGO electrode (D) Cycling performance for the obtained δ-MnO2/N-rGO catalyzed Li-O2 battery limited to 500 mAh/g.
Limited discharge and charge capacity of δ-MnO2/N-rGO with respect to cycle number 0.1 mA/cm2.
Electrochemical data obtained from Linear Sweep Voltammetry (LSV) curves.
Performance comparison of as synthesized electrocatalyst with different graphene based electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and experimental investigation of δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite for Li-O2 batteries applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among all the electrocatalysts for Lithium-Air battery (Li-air), Platinum Pt is the best performing cathode material. However, the high cost of noble Pt metal and scarcity nature impedes the use of Pt-based catalysts from being extensivity used in commercial applications. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an efficient and cost-effective alternate electrocatalyst to replace Pt-based materials in lithium-oxygen (Li-O2) battaries. In the present work, δ-MnO2/N-rGO composite (MNGC) has been synthesized by a simple non-template hydrothermal approach. MNGC with a porous wall structure composed of ultrathin nanosheets exhibits excellent electrochemical properties for oxidation–reduction reaction (ORR). MNGC can provide numerous pathways for abundant oxygen and electrolyte access to facilitate the mass transfer of lithium-ion. Such a well-designed structure offers the right electrocatalyst for the air cathode in lithium-oxygen (Li-O2) battaries. The prepared samples principal characteristics are analyzed, which verified the successful synthesis of sheet-like δ-MnO2 grown over the surface of nitrogen-doped reduced graphene oxide (N-rGO). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) results of MNGC showed enhanced ORR performance compared to MnO2 and N-rGO in terms of the half-wave potential, limiting current, and onset potential. MNGC electrode displayed superior cyclic performances of Li-air with a stable specific capacity, decreased overpotential, reversibility, and rate capability. Li-O2 battery was also tested with MNGN electrode for limited discharge capacity of 500 mAh/g, long-term cycling was achieved without electrolyte degradation. A high specific capacity of 5250 mAh/g was obtained at a high current density of 0.2 mA/cm2. The interlinked effect of δ–MnO2 and N–rGO for supporting the electrochemical interaction between O2 and Li is explained for improved columbic and energy efficiency of Li–O2 battery

ce:section.ce:para: Among various metal oxides, MnO2 has shown promising performance as a cathode electrocatalyst due to its low cost, natural abundance, high-energy density, environmental compatibility, and the possibility of porous microstructure, which enables high surface area and rapid ion transport [18–20]. However, MnO2 in the bulk form usually shows a small number of active sites on the surface; thus resists electronic and ionic diffusion, which generates a limited catalytic activity and rate in electrochemical energy storage systems. One of the alternative strategies to counter this issue is reducing active catalytic directions in the dimensions of MnO2 materials, as nanoscale MnO2 can reduce the time required for diffusion of ions and provide more catalytically active sites [21]. Numerous nanostructured MnO2-based materials have been developed which show extraordinary electrochemical performance for energy storage applications, such as lithium-ion batteries [22,23], lithium–oxygen (Li-O2) batteries [24–26], and supercapacitors [27–29], which constitutes a large demand for the transportation and other consumer related electronic industries [30–32].

To improve the electrochemical performance of MnO2 as cathode, various nanoscale MnO2 materials, which include nanowires [33], nanorods [26,34], nanotubes [35,36], hollow clews, [37] sheet-like MnO2 [38,39], and MnO2 nanoparticles [33,40], have been widely studied. All these studies present their findings on α-MnO2-based materials. The core idea behind all these developments is that α-MnO2 depicts good electrochemical performance when compared with other crystalline structures as concluded by Bruce et al., who thoroughly analyzed the catalytic activity of several manganese oxides, including MnO2, Mn2O3, and Mn3O4(α, β, γ, and λ phases) [41].

In this work, we have also developed various MnO2 nanostructures with proven superior electrochemical performance compared to the simple carbon electrode [42]. MnO2-based materials have been extensively studied as ORR cathode to enhance the electrochemical activity. In the δ-MnO2 polycrystals based morphology, K+ finds its place in the interlayer spacing of the 2-D lamellar structure of δ-MnO2 [43,44], which affects its electrochemical behavior to some extent. This shows that δ-MnO2 can act as a highly efficient catalyst. ORR activity of the MnO2 may be further increased when combined with high conductive materials. Adding MnO2 to a conductive substrate, such as graphene, seems to be an effective approach in solving some of the problems which are already discussed above [45,15]. In the last couple of years, several MnO2-based nanostructures have been synthesized and successfully used as electrodes for energy storage applications [46,47].

In order to modify the electrical conductivity of MnO2 based electrodes, continued efforts have been made on using a controlled composition of highly conductive carbon-based materials [17,48,49]. It has been observed that the interaction between metal oxides and carbon-based nanomaterials is usually loose, which results in the form of low-performing and non-durable catalysts. One of the approaches to increase the catalysts' efficiency and electrocatalytic activity is to grow the MnO2-based catalyst on a highly conductive support. On the other side, the focus has been shifted to graphene-based materials to improve their electrochemical proeprties [50,51–52] Nitrogen doping has become an important tool to improve the properties of graphene by enhancing its electrical conductivity and surface active sites. In our previous report, we have developed highly catalytic active nitrogen-doped carbon materials for ORR [53]. The sp2 based hybridized carbon materials have an abundance of free electrons, which makes them suitable catalyst materials for a reaction involving electrons such as ORR. But, all these free electrons are non-influencing, which renders them to be directly used in ORR. Upon nitrogen doping, electron-rich carbon nanostructures, the carbon electrons are activated by interaction with the lone-pair electrons from doped nitrogen; thus causing oxygen molecules reduction on the positively charged carbon atoms in the neighborhood of nitrogen.

Graphene is proven to be an efficient host for active nanomaterials due to the availability of large surface area, chemical stability, and flexibility. Furthermore, the preferred crystallographic orientation of MnO2 can be obtained due to interactions in the graphene substrate with a highly ordered structure. It is evident from the literature that in the past, the major focus has been on the synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped graphene (N-G) in a single phase [54,55]. But certain reports point out toward developing composite materials based on nitrogen-doped graphene and MnO2 with a good dispersion rate. For this reason, it has become an important consideration to design a balanced structure of MnO2, which is supported on carbon-based materials such as graphene to increase its catalytic activity.

There are few reports available on the application of ORR on δ-MnO2/N-rGO composite to the best of our knowledge. Here, we demonstrate a simple non-template hydrothermal method to synthesize a δ-MnO2/N-rGO composite (MNGC) with a porous wall structure composed of ultrathin nanosheets for ORR. These attractive architectures can supplement (a) numerous pathways for the access of electrolyte and oxygen to facilitate the fast diffusion of lithium ions within the electrode, (b) a large number of catalytically active sites, (c) intrinsically highly active catalyst that cause a product discharge that will resemble like a uniformly dispersed nanosheet-like structure.

Commercial graphene was obtained from Carbon nanomaterials technology Co. Ltd. and further treated for doping with nitrogen following the procedure explained previously [53]. The δ–MnO2 nanorods were synthesized on N-rGO by the conventional hydrothermal technique [42]. N-rGO was treated with KMnO4 using mortar and pestle, and the grounded mixture was eventually dispersed in 30 ml deionized water (DI water). For a typical synthesis of δ-MnO2/N-rGO, 0.2 g MnSO4•3H2O in 15 ml DI water, along with desired amounts of KMnO4 and N-rGO in Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The reaction mixture in the autoclave was kept in a preheated electric oven for 2 h at 140 °C. The obtained mixture was washed with DI water several times and finally dried in a vacuum oven to produce the final powder product of δ-MnO2/N-rGO.

The surface morphology and crystallographic properties of the synthesized samples were evaluated by field emission electron microscopy (FESEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), and X-ray diffraction technique (XRD). Raman Spectroscopy and Xray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to determine the chemical characteristics of the samples and to study the nitrogen doping phenomenon. The BET surface area and pore size distribution were measured using ASAB2020 (micrometrics Instuments).

To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity and performance of δ-MnO2/N-rGO, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and rotating disk electrode (RDE) techniques were used to obtain ORR polarization curves. A computerized potentiostat from CH Instruments (model number CHI 760 D) was used to complete the electrochemical studies using a three-electrode configuration. In a typical test, a Hg/HgO based reference electrode was used along with Pt wire as a counter electrode. Before any electrochemical test, the electrochemical cell was bubbled with oxygen (O2) for at least an hour. For the preparation of the working electrode for electrochemical measurements, in a typical scheme, the as-prepared δ-MnO2/N-rGO powder was mixed with carbon powder (Cabot Vulcan XC-72) at a 3:7 wt:wt ratio to provide reasonable electrical conductivity. 10 mg of as-prepared δ-MnO2/N-rGO with carbon powder was dispersed using ultrasonication in 150µL 5 wt.% Nafion-isopropyl alcohol solution. A quantity of 13.5 µL from the prepared suspension was drop cast on a glassy carbon substrate. A rotating disk electrode (RDE) was used to measure the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) characteristic using a glassy carbon substrate. For the measurements of ORR polarization curves, the LSVs were obtained in 0.1 M KOH saturated with O2 at a scan rate of 5 mVs−1 at 1600 rpm.

XRD characterization results of δ-MnO2/N-rGO are shown in Fig. 1 (A), which shows the reference peak for GO as well. Furthermore, the same spectra show the growth characteristics of pure δ-phase of MnO2 with monoclinic amorphous nanosheet structures (C2/m space group- JCPDS 44–0141). The layered structure of δ-MnO2 in the same spectra is confirmed by the presence of (001) and (002) peaks. Rietveld analysis is used to calculate the lattice parameters of δ-MnO2 phase, the values of which are a = 5.1 Å, b = 2.8 Å, and c = 7.1 Å. The calculated lattice parameters are comparable to standard values of similar phases, which are a = 5.15 Å, b = 2.84 Å, and c = 7.17 Å. Again referring to Fig. 1(A), the peak position at 2θ of 12.2° in the pattern of GO is from (001) plane of graphene oxide, which corresponds to a d-spacing of 0.72 nm, which confirms the oxidation of graphite flakes to graphite oxide. In addition to this, the XRD pattern for N-rGO indicates that GO has been reduced while the diffraction peaks at 2θ of 25° and 43° are attributed to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite-based structures. The peak position of (002) in the vicinity 2θ of 23° indicates the occurrence of a small domain with parallel stacking of graphene sheets, while the reflection of (100) at 2θ of 44˚ is attributed to the fibrous/spherical structures formed by Sp2 hybridized carbons [56,57]. We can observe that in nitrogen-doped graphene, the peaks are shifted towards higher 2θ values than undoped graphene, which indicates that nitrogen has been incorporated in GO interstices. Meanwhile, there was no observable shift in the (002) plane [56,58]. XRD can also be used to determine the interaction of MnO2 nanoparticles with nitrogen-doped graphene networks. A broad peak around 2θ of 24° is observed for δ-MnO2/N-rGO, which indicates the restacking of nitrogen-doped graphene sheets [59,60]. As soon as the MnO2 is incorporated, the peak at 2θ of 24° becomes weaker, and two new peaks appear at around 2θ of 36.8° and 66.7°. These two peaks correspond to the (111) and (312) peaks of MnO2, which further affirms that MnO2 nanoparticles are successfully introduced in N-rGO.

The Raman spectra of pristine and N-doped graphene are shown in Fig. 1(B). It shows two prominent peaks centered at 1346 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1 correspond to the D band (disordered carbon structure) and G band (characteristic of E2g mode of sp2 hybridized carbon), respectively [61]. The intensity ratio of the D to G band (ID/IG) is frequently used to estimate the defects in the carbon structure. The positive difference in (ID/IG) of N-doped graphene (1.42) with pristine graphene (1.40) suggests that defect density is significantly increased, which thus indicates the possibility of rapid electron transfer processes. Furthermore, the presence of the 2D and the combination of D+D’ bands in the spectrum of N-doped graphene indicates the existence of multi- and monolayer(s) graphene nanosheets [63].

To explore the chemical composition and confirm the N-doping, the XPS analysis of δ-MnO2/N-rGO were studied. Representative results are shown in Fig. 2 (A–D). As shown in the XPS survey spectrum of δ-MnO2/N-rGO (Fig. 2(A)), the presence of C1s, O1s, and N1s at 284.6, 531.5, and 401.2 eV, respectively, indicates the existence of C, O, and N elements. The observed C1s peak can be divided into three representative peaks (Fig. 2(B)) of –C–C–/–C=C–, –C=N–, and –C=O at 284.7, 285.6, and 289.1 eV, respectively [62]. While O1s peak can be divided into two representative peaks (Fig. 2(C) of C–O–C and N–O–C at 531.5 eV and 533.3 eV, respectively [63]. However, the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2(D)) shows two states of N in graphene basal planes, which are pyridinic–N and pyrrolic–N at 395.92 and 396.98 eV, respectively [64]. The appearance of a peak at 285.6 eV reflects the bonding between carbon and nitrogen atoms (–C=N–) confirms the successful N-doping via the hydrothermal treatment [62].

FESEM analyses were performed to study the morphology of the samples at different synthesis levels. The commercially obtained graphene clearly shows the monolayers of graphene sheets (Fig. 3 (A)), while after reduction and N-doping, the N-rGO surface has a wrinkled structure and layered thin platelet sheets (Fig. 3(B)). Moreover, in Fig. 3(C&D)), low and high magnification FESEM image of the δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite revealed the good combination of the N-rGO and δ-MnO2 sheets, which are uniformly distributed on the surface of graphene. SEM-EDX was used to further analyze the chemical composition of the δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite. Fig. 4 (A) shows the SEM image with corresponding EDX analysis Fig. 4(B). As shown in Fig. 4(B), C, Mn, O, and N are the major elements in the nanocomposite. The atomic and weight percentages of the δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite obtained by SEM-EDX are summarized in Fig. 4(B, inset).

The surface area and pore size distribution of the δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite were analyzed by the N2 adsorption-desorption measurments. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm in Fig. 4(C) shows type IV adsorption isotherm based on the IUPAC classification which indicates the formation of mesopores in the product with the surface area of 47.23 m2/g. While synthesized δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite have an average pore size in the range of 1–1.5 nm determined by BJH technique (Fig. 4(C, inset)).

Rotating disc electrode techniques (RDE) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were used to study the electrocatalytic activity of N-rGO and the δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanocomposite for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). For comparison, CV and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) of commercial graphene and MnO2 also have been measured. ORR's catalyst performance can be quantitatively estimated from the onset of reduction peak potentials in CV curves. Fig. 5 (A) shows the CV curves for ORR on all synthesized samples in 0.1 M KOH aqueous solution saturated with oxygen, at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. It can also be seen in Fig. 5(A) that oxygen-saturated solutions when tested for N-rGO and δ-MnO2/N-rGO show quasi rectangular voltammograms, which is an indication of high surface area carbon [67]. The onset potentials of N-rGO and δ-MnO2/N-rGO composite (MNGC) are −0.15 and −0.1 V, respectively, while it is −0.14 V for MnO2. The MNGC exhibits a significant positive shift of onset potential than MnO2 and commercial graphene. It is clear from the figures that the onset potential of MNGC is less negative than MnO2 and commercial graphene due to fast reaction kinetics. Due to a positive shift in onset potential and fast reaction kinetics, it can be concurred that MNGC is a better performing ORR electrocatalytic [68].

Fig. 5(B) shows the linear sweep voltammetry (LSVs) curves using RDE at a rotation speed of 1600 rpm to investigate the electrocatalytic activity of MnO2, N-rGO, and δ-MnO2/N-rGO to compare ORR electroactivity. From Fig. 5(B), δ-MnO2/N-rGO showed an onset potential of −0.05 V with a limiting current of 0.88 mA, while these values were −0.08 V and −0.14 V for MnO2 and N-rGO, respectively. LSV curve of δ-MnO2/N-rGO presented a much more positive onset potential of 30 mV in comparison with MnO2 and N-rGO, the summarized data is presented in Table 1 . The significant positive shift in the MNGC relative to MnO2 and N-rGO explicitly shows that MNGC is electro-catalytically more active. The high limiting current and positive shift in onset potential of δ-MnO2/N-rGO could be attributed to superior ORR catalytic activity of sheet-like δ-MnO2 and the porous structure of N-rGO composite electrode, which permits easy electrolyte permeation and oxygen diffusion, besides the effective conductive network provided by N-doped graphene.

The catalytic activity for ORR of δ-MnO2/N-rGO in air cathode based on Li-O2 was tested in a Swagelok™ cell with 1 M Lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI (TEGDME)) solution and with Li metal electrode (anode). The performance of the Li-O2 cells was measured galvanostatically at an applied potential of 2.00–4.30 V. Furthermore, the charge/discharge curves for the Li–O2 battery evaluated at a current density of 0.1 mA/cm2 is shown in Fig. 6 (A). The initial discharge begins at the reference open circuit potential (OCP) of 3.2 V with a steady level at 2.70 V and arrives at a full value of discharge capacity at 3300 mAh/g. Retention capacity is also achieved upon charging with a maximum of 95% with a cap of 3150 mAh/g and a potential gap of 0.6 V, which is way lower than the value reported previously [65,66]. The second discharge capacity is 2750 mAh/g, the third and fourth discharge capacities have been significantly increased and measured at 3180 and 3250 mAh/g, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(B).

Although it is observed that capacity increase for the first few cycles, i.e., 2–4, but afterward, the specific capacity starts decreasing. The fifth cycle was measured to be 3050 mAh/g, and then a steady downward trend is observed. However, the discharge capacity stays above 1400 mAh/g even after 7th cycle, as shown in Fig. 6(D). The specific capacity for cycle number is also shown in Fig. 6(C), which is comparable to other reported works [25,67]. It was discussed that this type of unusual discharge capacity response was due to their catalysts ORR activity, which promotes reactions near the catalysts active sites, which results in an increase in cycle activity and decrease of the overpotential [67]. Higher current densities were also used to measure the rate capability of synthesized δ-MnO2/N-rGO. The values of 0.2 and 0.3 mA/cm2 were used to compare to 0.1 mA/cm2 (Fig. 6(A)). Catalyzed Li-O2 battery cycle rate capacities are 5250–1150 mAh/g for 0.2 and 0.3 mA/cm2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(A). We obtain a much better specific capacity of 5250 mAh /g at a high current density of 0.2 mA /cm2.

The cell cyclability was also tested for a limited discharge capacity; of 500 mAh/g, and the properties related to the cycling of the catalyst were measured for 45 cycles. As shown in Fig. 6(D), a fine cycling property with no considerable change in the discharge potential but an increase in the over-charge potential after 35 cycles has been observed, which is the key issue associated with Li-air batteries. Table 2
shows that the nanocomposite syntheized in this work has a comparable electrocatalytic performance to those of previously reported electrocatalyst.

The charge/discharge potential was also measured at the same limited discharge capacity of 500 mAh/g as shown in Fig 7 , as a function of the cycle number; although a slight increase in the load potential is observed at approximately 35 cycles, the potential persists up to 50 cycles. This clearly shows that as-synthesized δ-MnO2/N-rGO nanostructures have good catalytic efficiency toward ORR in Li-O2 battery. The increase in overpotential with the increase in cycle number causes the deterioration of the output in the cathode and the deterioration of electrolytes, which results in the batterys poor round trip efficiency [68,69]. This is one of the major challenges that limit the Li-O2 batterys efficiency. However, in our case, we have observed a decrease in overpotential in each cycle of the first few cycles (complete cycles), as shown in Fig. 6(B). It was also observed that after the first five cycles, the overpotential was not greatly increased. The Li-O2 battery's cyclability was also tested at a restricted discharge rate; the charge/discharge potential difference, ΔV of δ-MnO2/N-rGO catalyst was lower and more stable, thus maintaining 100% charging/discharge power efficiency over 50 cycles as shown in Fig. 7.

One important justification for enhancing Li-O2 battery efficiency is the total decomposition of discharge products on the electrode surface. Much work has been introduced to improve electrocatalysts for the decomposition and formation reactions of Li2O2. Still, most catalysts also enable the decomposition and formation of carbonate salts on the surface of electrodes as well as the decomposition of the electrolyte [70,71]. However, the herein synthesized δ-MnO2/N-rGO electrocatalyst appears to participate actively only in the Li2O2 decomposition and formation reaction. The higher columbic charge efficiency of the initial five cycles between discharge and charging capacities Fig. 6(B) indicates the correct decomposition and formation of the reaction products.

In this work, a nanocomposite electrocatalyst was synthesized by growing δ-MnO2 directly on the nitrogen-doped graphene; δ-MnO2/N-rGO shows a sheet-like structure composed of ultrathin nanosheets. The electrocatalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction ORR significantly increases with MNGC, which is better performing as compared to the MnO2 and N-rGO. Batteries with δ-MnO2/N-rGO (MNGC) electrode can deliver a good discharge capacity of about 3300 mAh/gat a discharge plateau of 2.7 V and 0.1 mA/cm2. For a fixed specific capacity of 500mAh/g, the battery can show a stable cycling rate for up to 500 cycles before any cathode or electrolyte degradation is observed.

The electrochemical attributes of MNCG electrodes for Li-O2 battery indicate excellent characteristics due to high oxygen reduction reaction ORR catalytic activity, which eventually permit easy wetting and oxygen diffusion. Furthermore, the presence of carbon atoms with adjacent nitrogen atoms increases the positive charge density, and their interaction leads to higher ORR activity. These results show that MNGC accelerates the diffusion-controlled processes with excellent energy conversion efficiency and faster reaction kinetics, which is largely associated with favorable N-graphite species and porous surface morphology. Nitrogen doping causes structural defects and provides a pair of electrons, increasing the lattice negative charge. The synthesized δ-MnO2/N-rGO (MNGC) is a promising candidate for air electrodes of batteries, especially metal-air batteries such as Li-air.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: On behalf of all authors, the corresponding author states that there is no conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalysts for lithium–air batteries: current status and challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zahoor, A.; Ghouri, Z.K.; Hashmi, S.; Raza, F.; Ishtiaque, S.; Nadeem, S.; Ullah, I.; Nahm, K.S., Electrocatalysts for lithium–air batteries: current status and challenges. ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng.2019, 7 (17), 14288–14320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation mechanisms of Pt/C fuel cell catalysts under simulated start–stop conditions
